Handstand — three-subject comparison

LoicSeppeyLucas · 16 valid takes (7 / 5 / 4)  ·  Motive @ 360 Hz  ·  Per guide §6.5 (static balance): hold span detected dynamically, no outlier filtering
Three handstands. Median hold duration: Loic 1.3 s, Seppey 0.8 s, Lucas 0.9 s. The headline finding isn't who holds longest — the difference is real but small — but the three subjects use noticeably different motor strategies. Loic has the most vertical body axis but lets it drift across the floor. Seppey has the strongest shoulder push (chest elevated 27% of arm-length above the shoulders) despite the largest body tilt. Lucas barely pushes through the shoulders (7% lift) and has shorter, less stable holds. Each subject's signature spans a different combination of these three axes — verticality, oscillation, and shoulder portance — which a single 'hold duration' summary would miss entirely.

1. Detected hold spans

2. Hip horizontal motion during the hold

3. Body-axis tilt during the hold

4. Shoulder portance — symmetry and elevation

5. Within-subject consistency

6. Summary metrics

Seppey0.79 ± 0.572.197.6 ± 3.55.1 ± 5.015.8° ± 3.927% ± 85.2 ± 2.8 mm51.7%
Lucas0.86 ± 0.391.399.8 ± 2.15.0 ± 2.19.5° ± 5.67% ± 04.2 ± 1.0 mm43.0%
16 valid takes (Loic 7, Seppey 5, Lucas 4). Hold duration shown as median ± std (typical) and best (longest single take) per guide §11. Cells tinted: green = better, red = worse. Hold-duration CV is the within-subject coefficient of variation.

7. Key observations

  • Hold — typical (s): Loic highest (1.28 ± 0.49) — Seppey lowest (0.79 ± 0.57)
  • Hold — best (s): Loic highest (2.22) — Lucas lowest (1.39)
  • Drift (cm): Seppey lowest (7.6 ± 3.5) — Loic highest (29.4 ± 17.1)
  • Oscillation (cm): Lucas lowest (5.0 ± 2.1) — Loic highest (7.1 ± 3.0)
  • Body tilt mid-50% (°): Loic lowest (4.1° ± 6.2) — Seppey highest (15.8° ± 3.9)
  • Shoulder lift (% arm): Seppey highest (27% ± 8) — Lucas lowest (7% ± 0)
  • Shoulder L/R asymmetry: Loic lowest (1.7 ± 1.1 mm) — Seppey highest (5.2 ± 2.8 mm)
  • Hold-duration CV: Loic lowest (36.3%) — Seppey highest (51.7%)
Synthesis. Four findings. (i) Hold duration: Loic typically 1.3s (best 2.2s), Seppey 0.8s (best 2.2s), Lucas 0.9s (best 1.4s). Loic and Seppey's best takes are nearly identical (~2.2s); the gap is in the median, i.e. in reliability. (ii) Sway needs decomposition: Loic looks 2× worse than the others on raw sway range, but that's almost entirely drift. After detrending, all three have comparable oscillation (~5-7 cm). The three subjects have similar moment-to-moment balance noise; what differs is whether they drift across the floor. (iii) Body-axis tilt (measured Hip→Neck so cervical motion doesn't pollute the trunk verticality): Loic ~7°, Lucas ~11°, Seppey ~18°. Loic is the most vertical. (iv) Shoulder portance reverses some of the ordering: Loic ~22% lift, Seppey ~27% (the highest, despite the worst tilt), Lucas ~7% (essentially no push). This completes a richer picture: Loic = vertical-and-stable but drifts; Seppey = strong shoulder engagement but tilts; Lucas = passive posture with shorter holds. Three subjects, three distinct technical signatures rather than a single performance ranking. Per guide §6.5, no outlier filtering was applied — high within-subject variability is part of the balance signature, not contamination.
Limits of this analysis. Limits of this analysis. n = 1 subject per group; the comparison describes Loic, Seppey and Lucas individually, not any broader category of expert vs novice gymnasts. The number of takes also varies (Loic 7, Seppey 7, Lucas 4) — a single long hold has more weight in Lucas's median than in the others.

Data-quality probe: worst NaN coverage on a key bone (Hip / Chest / Head / hands / feet) inside the hold span was 0.00% on (longest consecutive gap: 0 frames). Takes rejected by the per-take validity check: Seppey_1 (head not inverted (min Y = 1.52)); Seppey_5 (no stable hold ≥ 0.5s detected).

The Motive skeleton names in the source files do not match the filenames (Seppey_1's skeleton is named Loic_expert; all of Lucas's skeletons are named 'Julien codes'; Seppey_2_002's skeleton is also 'Julien codes'). This is a labelling artifact from re-using the Motive solver across sessions and does not affect the underlying mocap data. Subject attribution in this analysis is derived from the filename, which is the reliable signal here. Seppey_2_002 was confirmed to be an independent take (different captureStartTime, different duration, different bone count from Seppey_2) and kept as a valid Seppey performance.

Diagnostic outlier flags (not excluded, per §6.5): 10 takes deviated by >40% from their subject median on hold-duration or sway-path. For static balance this is informative variability, not contamination.
Pipeline: mocap.py + report.py from the project knowledge base. Family: §6.5 static posture & balance. POI = onset of stable inverted hold (feet within 10 cm of their max for ≥ 0.5 s). ROI = the hold span itself. Vertical Y, quaternions (x, y, z, w). Smoothing: Savitzky-Golay window 21, polynomial 3.
